[165:16]As to Iran, I think that Iran underestimates the peril they are in.

[165:25]They are engaging the United States in talks, the Ayatollah has given permission for the

[165:30]president and the foreign minister to engage the United States in talks.

[165:34]But they're not budging.

[165:36]They're not budging on enrichment.

[165:37]There's no sign that they're budging.

[165:39]They're agreeing to some compromises, but their red line remains, which is they're not giving

[165:45]up enrichment.

[165:50]And they think that they are safe, because I think they underestimate the willingness

[165:55]of the United States to go all in.

[165:59]They believe that in order for the United States to topple their regime, the United

[166:03]States will need a ground force, will need to invade.

[166:07]And they don't believe the United States will do that.

[166:09]And they also think that any protracted war with Iran will bleed into the midterms.

[166:21]But I think that's a miscalculation. I think that the president is looking for a quick

[166:27]and decisive win here. I think as we saw in Venezuela, he is willing to act decisively

[166:34]and he's willing to act creatively. He's throwing out that old playbook. It isn't 2003,

[166:46]And Trump, although he thinks that regime changes sometimes necessary and I think that

[166:51]Trump loves this maximum pressure strategy that sometimes overflows into military action,

[166:59]it is a different playbook.

[167:01]I would call it like a form of smart power.

[167:07]When she was Secretary of State, it's not soft power, and it's not exactly the most blunt

[167:13]form of hard power, but it's hard power applied in a smart way, like what happened in Venezuela.

[167:19]The conventional thinking in Venezuela is that we're going to invade or that we're gonna do a

[167:25]major air campaign and we're gonna fight their entire military and then we're gonna kill everyone

[167:30]in the government. And Trump threw that old playbook out. It wasn't that.

[167:36]It was an abduction. It was an extradition. We just went in using very unconventional

[167:42]tactics and unconventional, perhaps weapons. And by overwhelming their air defenses, we

[167:49]were able to get special forces in there, pick up the guy and take him out. And then

[167:59]We are now working with the remnant of the decapitated government.

[168:04]The regime remains in place, the security architecture remains in place.

[168:09]We kidnapped their leader, which is just like the act of war to end all acts of war.

[168:16]And you have our energy secretary posing for a photo op with the vice president serving

[168:25]It's unconventional. It's different. It's a different playbook. And I think that Iran,

[168:30]they are looking at the United States in the same way that they would have looked at the

[168:34]Biden administration or the Obama administration or the Bush administration, but it isn't like

[168:39]that. It's a new sheriff and Trump doesn't play on that timeline. That's not his tempo.

[168:53]And the question now, like I said, is, what is it going to be?

[168:56]And according to this article, it says that Trump is thinking about doing a very limited

[173:00]And the reason why I say this, and this was sort of my prediction last night,

[173:05]is that the calculus about this strike centers on the fact that regime change is really not desirable and not feasible.

[173:30]Naval power is insufficient.

[173:33]And we don't even have a lot of it there.

[173:48]to feed a million man army, and whatever remnant there would be from the Revolutionary Guard

[179:14]it is desirable for us. Why? Because with a deal, the Iranian regime can survive. That's

[179:26]Sanctions relief means their currency recovers, their economy recovers.
